coredata.dc:title: Kinetic modelling of thermal decomposition in lithium-ion battery components during thermal runaway

coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghi, Hosein
Restuccia, Francesco
coredata.dc:description: This study presents kinetic models for the thermal decomposition of 18650-type lithium-ion battery components during thermal runaway, including the SEI layer, anode, separator, cathode, electrolyte, and binder. The decomposition kinetics were sourced from the literature. The approach used inverse modelling, employing a Genetic Algorithm to estimate kinetic and stoichiometric parameters. Experimental thermogravimetry data from the literature served as the reference benchmarks. The optimisation errors ranged from 0.039 % to 1.531 % , and the algorithm performed well in terms of reaction temperatures, with errors between 0.51 % and 11.07 % . The models were validated for calculating the mass loss of a full cell at 100 % state of charge during thermal runaway. The early stages of thermal runaway, including the decomposition of the anode and separator, were considered in an electrochemical-thermal simulation of charge/discharge cycling using PyBaMM solver. The results showed that these decompositions could advance temperature and voltage profiles by 0.07 C over 20 cycles, aiding early prediction of thermal runaway in battery management systems. This work introduces novel models to calculate mass losses, identify reactions, quantify heat release, and estimate thickness or volume reductions in battery components during thermal runaway.

head.ce:title: Kinetic modelling of thermal decomposition in lithium-ion battery components during thermal runaway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents kinetic models for the thermal decomposition of 18650-type lithium-ion battery components during thermal runaway, including the SEI layer, anode, separator, cathode, electrolyte, and binder. The decomposition kinetics were sourced from the literature. The approach used inverse modelling, employing a Genetic Algorithm to estimate kinetic and stoichiometric parameters. Experimental thermogravimetry data from the literature served as the reference benchmarks. The optimisation errors ranged from 0.039 % to 1.531 % , and the algorithm performed well in terms of reaction temperatures, with errors between 0.51 % and 11.07 % . The models were validated for calculating the mass loss of a full cell at 100 % state of charge during thermal runaway. The early stages of thermal runaway, including the decomposition of the anode and separator, were considered in an electrochemical-thermal simulation of charge/discharge cycling using PyBaMM solver. The results showed that these decompositions could advance temperature and voltage profiles by 0.07 C over 20 cycles, aiding early prediction of thermal runaway in battery management systems. This work introduces novel models to calculate mass losses, identify reactions, quantify heat release, and estimate thickness or volume reductions in battery components during thermal runaway.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are crucial in the transition towards cleaner and more sustainable energy solutions. Their versatility, high energy density, and rechargeable nature make them indispensable in various industries, including consumer electronics, electric transportation, renewable energy storage, and more. As environmental sustainability is prioritised and efforts are made to reduce reliance on fossil fuels, the role of lithium-ion batteries in energy storage is anticipated to grow.

Although lithium-ion batteries offer numerous advantages, they also present significant challenges, with one of the most prominent being the safety hazards linked to the risk of thermal runaway and fires. Thermal runaway could occur due to three main mechanisms: electrical, thermal, and mechanical abuse [1]. Each of these mechanisms could lead to self-sustaining exothermic reactions occurring in sequence or parallel, including decomposition of battery components, internal short circuits (ISCs), generation of flammable gases, and ultimately fire or explosion.

The components involved in thermal runaway are the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer, anode, cathode, electrolyte, and binder [2]. The SEI layer is a thin layer that develops on the surface of the graphite anode during the operation of a battery. This layer forms as a result of irreversible electrochemical reactions within the battery’s electrolyte, leading to a loss of battery capacity. The SEI layer acts as a protective barrier, preventing further reaction between the electrolyte and the anode, thus ensuring stable and reliable battery performance over multiple charge and discharge cycles. The electrolyte is a mixture of organic carbonates that serve as solvents for lithium salts such as LiPF
6
and LiTFSI. Common carbonates used in electrolytes include ethylene carbonate (EC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), propylene carbonate (PC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and dimethyl carbonate (DMC). The anode is the negative active material, commonly made from graphite or lithium titanium oxide (LTO) with the chemical formula Li
4
Ti
5
O12, which has been shown to have better storage-life performance compared to graphite [3]. The cathode is the positive active material and in LIBs, it is made of a lithium metal oxide compound, such as lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO
2
), lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO
4
), or a nickel-based compound such as Li(Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1). Binders are electronically inactive materials but they can have influences on the battery performance and are also involved in thermal decomposition. Binders exist in both the anode and cathode electrodes. Different materials such as polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and alginate have been studied in the literature [4]. PVDF has traditionally been used for both the cathode and the anode of commercial LIBs due to its good adhesive properties and electrochemical stability, but it has been associated with some drawbacks, such as high costs and difficult recycling. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is a newer binder material produced from natural cellulose which is not associated with the limitations of PVDF [5]. These binders can be used interchangeably, but it has been shown that CMC binders could have negative effects on some cathode materials [6] while they performed well with some others [4]. Hence, using PVDF for the cathode and CMC for the anode [7] would be a more efficient design.

In the battery abuse conditions, the stability of the SEI layer is compromised, leading to different failure mechanisms depending on the type of abuse. In the case of mechanical abuse, the SEI layer is broken down, whereas with other abuse mechanisms, this layer undergoes an exothermic decomposition process. The removal of the SEI layer typically occurs at temperatures ranging from 69 to 120 °C [2], followed by exothermic reactions between the electrolyte and the graphite anode triggering around 100 °C to 130 °C. As the temperature increases to 135–200 °C, the separator melts or shrinks, potentially leading to ISCs when the cathode and anode come into contact. ISCs act as accelerators in the thermal runaway process. However, it is important to note that the thermal runaway process can still occur even without ISCs if there is sufficient heat [8]. In the next stage, the cathode begins to decompose and react with the electrolyte at temperatures ranging from 100 to 500 °C, depending on the cathode material. Following this, electrolyte decomposition occurs at temperatures greater than 200 °C, and finally the binder reacts with the cathode material at temperatures exceeding 260 °C. Fig. 1 provides a brief description of these stages. The entire process is associated with the generation of both nonflammable and flammable gases, which have been identified in several experiments in the literature. The primary species include CO, CO
2
, CH
4
, C
2
H
4
, C
2
H
6
, and H
2
[9–11]. However, minor species such as C
2
H
2
[12], C
6
H
6
, and C
6
H
5
CH
3
[13] have also been reported.

The thermal decomposition of each component has been extensively studied in the literature using available techniques such as thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), and temperature-resolved X-ray diffraction (TR-XRD). These techniques are instrumental in measuring key parameters, including mass loss, mass-loss rate, heat release rate, and identifying the released species. The results were then utilised to analyse the thermal stability of the materials, identify the number of primary components in the decomposition process, and predict the decomposition reactions.

The decomposition of the SEI layer has been shown to occur in two dominant stages, with 2% and 3% mass losses observed. It was also found that the exothermic decomposition of both the SEI and the anode material initiates self-heating [14]. The thermal decomposition of anodes has attracted attention in various contexts, including the study of new materials [15,16], the effects of ageing [17], the impact of lithium plating on the anode [18], and the modelling of thermal runaway [19,20]. The investigated materials include reduced graphene oxide [15], graphite [17–20], as well as silicon/carbon composite materials [16]. The decomposition results for anode showed one major reaction with 5% [17] to 25% [18,19] mass loss.

Barkholtz et al. [14] disassembled the separator from fully charged commercial 18650-type batteries manufactured by LG Chem, A123 Systems, and Panasonic, and conducted thermogravimetry analysis. Each separator exhibited different behaviour in thermal decomposition, with mass losses ranging from 17% to 33% and peak heat releases ranging from 0.6 to 1.4 mW/mg.

In an early study on positive electrodes, Wang et al. [21] investigated the thermal stability of LiCoO
2
cathodes at 100% state of charge (SOC) using TGA. They identified four mass loss stages. Stage one, with 1% mass loss, was assigned to LiPF
6
, while stage two, with 3.76% mass loss, was attributed to the decomposition of the PVDF binder and the SEI formed on the electrode’s surface. Stages three and four were identified as acetylene carbon black decomposition with 5.48% mass loss and the decomposition of Co
3
O
4
with 3.36% mass loss, respectively. Wang et al. [21] underscored the crucial role of thermal stability in reducing fire risks. Veluchamy et al. [22] examined LCO cathodes at different SOC levels and found that higher SOC levels led to increased mass loss, emphasising the SOC’s impact on cathode stability. In their study, three different stages were identified. The first stage was the evaporation of the electrolyte solvent or a combination thereof, along with weight loss due to oxidation reactions in stages two and three. In Ref. [23], the thermal decomposition of LCO cathodes and electrolytes was studied. For the cathode, a two-stage decomposition was identified. The first stage led to a 7.4% mass loss, with 1% assigned to the decomposition of the PVDF binder, and a further 5.4% mass loss was measured for the second stage. In Ref. [24], three stages for cathode decomposition were identified: (I) loss of LiPF
6
or other contaminants, (II) degradation of PVDF and (III) acetylene carbon black. This approach was also employed for cathode selection [24] and manufacturing [25]. For instance, NMC cathodes exhibited the poorest thermal performance and the highest risk of thermal runaway compared to LFP and LMO cathodes in Ref. [24].

The thermal stability and fire hazards of electrolytes have been extensively studied. When comparing organic solvent mixtures, including EC/DMC, EC/DEC, EC/EMC, EC/DEC/DMC, EC/DMC/EA, and EC/PC/DMC, cyclic carbonates (e.g., EC and PC) outperformed linear carbonates (e.g., DMC, DEC, and EMC) [26]. Additionally, it was shown that the addition of ionic liquids to these mixtures improved the thermal stability of the electrolyte and prevented ignition [27].

Bertilsson et al. [28] studied the thermal decomposition of single components and extracted electrolyte from a prismatic cell. Analysing the emissions from the extracted electrolyte, they found three stages in TGA, including the evaporation of linear carbonates, salt decomposition, and finally the evaporation of cyclic carbonates. Studies on the thermal decomposition of electrolytes have shown that a significant portion, ranging from 80 %
[29] to 100 %
[28], of the mass is released. Regarding the combustion hazards of electrolytes, studies have shown that volatile electrolyte components dominate these hazards [30]. In another study by Huang et al. [31], it was demonstrated that adding ethyl acetate (EA) to the solvent mixture accelerated the fire development process due to higher heat fluxes.

Research on the thermal stability of binders is limited to a few works. Kriston et al. [19] analysed the thermal stability of anode and cathode binders extracted from a fully charged cell and then modelled the heat flow of the components based on TGA and DSC data. Li et al. [29] studied a vinylidene fluoride-based composite electrolyte as the cathode binder and, in addition to analysing the battery’s performance, compared its thermal stability with conventional PVDF binders.

As reviewed, the thermal stability of battery components has been extensively studied and analysed experimentally in the literature. However, there is still a lack of kinetic models for accurately modelling the thermal decomposition of these components during thermal runaway. Such models are crucial, as they can be used to calculate mass loss or be employed in electrochemical-thermal simulations of cells.

This work focuses on the inverse modelling of the thermal decomposition of the 18650-type cell components including SOC-dependant (anode and cathode) and non-SOC-dependant. The SOC-dependant components were studied at 100 % SOC. The 18650-type battery was chosen due to its wide applications and the well-documented literature on its physical behaviour during thermal runaway and the 100 % SOC was considered as this state poses a higher risk of thermal runaway and more severe subsequent hazards compared to other operational SOCs (0 ≤ SOC ≤ 100) [1].

The structure of the current study is as follows. First, the decomposition kinetics of the components, including the SEI layer, anode, separator, cathode, electrolyte, and binder of cells with LCO, LFP, and NMC cathodes, were analysed (Section 2). Next, a Genetic Algorithm (GA) was employed to estimate the kinetic and stoichiometric parameters based on TGA data obtained from the literature, and the resulting models were presented (Sections 3 and 4). Finally, these models were used to simulate the mass loss of a cell during thermal runaway and were integrated into electrochemical-thermal simulations, where calculations depend on the mass or thickness of the components (Section 5).

This section presents the thermal decomposition kinetics of the battery components, including SEI layer, anode, separator, cathode, and electrolyte. The decomposition behaviours are discussed based on existing TGA experiments in the literature. In TGA, the sample’s mass is typically very small, often just a few milligrams. The flow rate of gases such as nitrogen or air into the oven markedly exceeds the release rate of gaseous products from the degrading solid. As a result, transport effects and thermal gradients within the solid can be safely ignored [32]. Hence, the mass yield/loss rate of the solid species i with respect to the original component can be written in a general format as

To make the current study most applicable to high-risk scenarios and to simplify the complexities of the thermal runaway process, the following assumptions were made:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Chemical interactions between components during thermal runaway were not considered.
The most common component materials and the generally accepted decomposition kinetics were studied.
The leakage of components from the cell was ignored.
The ageing effects were ignored and fresh materials were considered.

ce:section.ce:para:
Investigating the decomposition of the SEI layer, Zhao et al. [34] assembled a cell using natural graphite powder as the negative electrode, an EC/DMC-based LiPF
6
electrolyte, a lithium foil as the positive electrode and a polypropylene film as separator. The graphite powder was mixed with PVDF binder and dissolved in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone. Subsequently, the slurry was coated onto a porous copper current collector. After charge/discharge cycles, the cell was disassembled, and the SEI layer was extracted for TGA at a heating rate of 5
o
C/min. The test revealed that the SEI fully decomposed up to 500 °C, with the decomposition consisting of two dominant steps. First, lithium alkyl carbonate (
CH
2
OCO
2
Li
) decomposed and produced lithium formaldehyde (
Li
2
C
2
H
4
O
2
) and lithium oxalate (
Li
2
C
2
O
4
). Then, it was followed by the decomposition of lithium oxalate. This process was also associated with CO
2
production [9,34]. The decomposition kinetic of the SEI layer, the mass yield/loss rates, and the total mass-loss rate of SEI layer are presented in Table 1.

As reviewed earlier, the thermal decomposition of the anode has been studied in several works [14–20]. In the current study, the research of Barkholtz et al. [14] was specifically utilised because the anodes were extracted from commercial 18650-type cells with LCO, LFP and NMC cathodes at different SOCs, including 100 % SOC at 4.2 V. In Ref. [14], the thermal stability of the extracted anodes was tested using TGA at 15
o
C/min, and they were further analysed by DSC and TR-XRD. It was observed in Ref. [14] that the extracted anodes (with LCO, LFP and NMC cathodes) had an onset of exothermic decomposition at 80–90 °C. The DSC and TR-XRD measurements revealed two reactions: the transition of LiC
6
to LiC12, followed by the transition of LiC12 to LiC18, with some unidentified residues remaining, R
anode,1
and R
anode,2
. The reactions at the anode were shown to produce CO; however, this could not be directly attributed to anode decomposition. Instead, it could result from other reactions, such as the reduction of CO
2
by intercalated lithium at the anode or the reaction of carbon with oxygen at high temperatures (combustion) [9]. The kinetic and the mass yield/loss rates in anode decomposition are presented in Table 1.

The thermal decomposition of the separator was investigated by Barkholtz et al. [14], with the extraction procedure detailed in Section 2.2. According to the information provided in Refs. [14,35], the separator material was polypropylene. The TGA/DSC results for the separator, as reported in Ref. [14], revealed a single relative peak for LG and Panasonic cells, and two peaks for A123 cell, indicative of one- and two-step reactions, respectively. However, since no specific kinetics are available for the separator, the thermal decomposition was assumed to follow general kinetic mechanisms, producing residue and volatiles. Furthermore, no specific gas generation has been associated with the separator in the literature [2,35]. The decomposition kinetics of the separators are presented in Table 1.

For the thermal decomposition analysis of LCO cathode, Wang et al. [21] conducted TGA on fully delithiated cathode materials at a rate of 10
o
C/min. Their analysis revealed four mass loss stages, wherein only one stage was attributed to the cathode material, while the remaining stages were assigned to the electrolyte, binder, and SEI layer. In the TGA conducted by Veluchamy et al. [22] at a heating rate of 5
o
C/min, three different regions were detected for the cathode. However, the kinetic formulation was presented in a one-step format. In the analyses conducted by Lee et al. [23], TGA was performed on a fully charged LCO cathode at a rate of 10
o
C/min, revealing two significant decomposition steps attributed to the binder and Li
x
CoO 2 , with the decomposition of Li
x
CoO
2
determined to occur via a one-step reaction. Chen et al. [36] extracted LCO cathode material from an 18650-type cell and identified two components, LiCoO
2
and Co
3
O 4 , participating in the TGA analysis conducted at 5
o
C/min. Consequently, they proposed a two-step kinetic model for LCO cathode decomposition. This two-step reaction was also suggested by Barkholtz [14], who identified Li
x
CoO
2
and Co
3
O
4
as the major components. As observed, the kinetics derived from the TGA data encompassed more than one reaction, suggesting a multi-step kinetic model as the most suitable. The most comprehensive kinetic model, consisting of three steps, was presented in Refs. [35,37], where the decomposition of CoO was also considered alongside Li
x
CoO
2
and Co
3
O
4
with oxygen production. The three-step kinetic model was considered in the present study and is outlined in Table 1.

The thermal decomposition of LFP cathode was investigated by Barkholtz et al. [14]. In Ref. [14], the sample was extracted from a fully charged 18650-type cell and subsequently washed. DSC measurements were conducted at a heating rate of 20
o
C/min, revealing that the material remained stable until 300 °C. However, an exothermic peak was observed around 500–550 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of the cathode into Fe
2
P
2
O
7
and Fe
7
(PO
4
)
6
. Although oxygen production was not assumed during LFP cathode decomposition in this study, some hypotheses suggest that it may occur, albeit without substantial evidence [35]. The decomposition kinetics of the LFP cathode is presented in Table 1.

The decomposition of fully delithiated NMC cathode initiated with phase transition from a layer structure (
R 3 m
) to a disordered spinel structure (
F d 3 m
) at ∼ 200 °C [19,38] which was associated with oxygen production. With further heating, NMC cathode reacted electrolyte produced metallic nickel and cobalt monoxide. At temperatures up to 600 °C, more intense oxygen release was observed and at temperatures above 600 °C, the lattice oxygen was observed [19]. However, this kinetic has been suggested when the electrolyte contained LiPF
6
, and the cathode was made of equal fractions of Ni, Co and Mn and changing any of the parameters could change the decomposition reaction [19,35]. For instance, with more nickel, the triggering temperature decreased and more oxygen was released. Here, the decomposition kinetic of NMC(111) was considered and is presented in Table 1.

The thermal decomposition of commercial electrolytes was investigated in Refs. [27,28]. Chang et al. [27] conducted TGA for EC/DMC LiPF
6
at 10
o
C/min. The process was found to occur in three steps: from 28 °C to 80 °C, from 80 °C to 160 °C, and from 160 °C to 550 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of DMC (
C
3
H
6
O
3
), EC (
C
3
H
4
O
3
) and LiPF
6
, respectively. In Ref. [28], the electrolyte was obtained from a commercial prismatic cell through a hole on the sealing. A similar TGA was conducted and the electrolyte was identified to be made of LiPF
6
in EC/DMC/EA solvent, revealing three decomposition steps. The decomposition of DMC and EA (
C
4
H
8
O
2
) occurred from room temperature to 77 °C, followed by the decomposition of EC, happening around 100 °C, and LiPF
6
in the temperature range of 80 °C to 160 °C. The electrolyte decomposition was associated with the generation of CH
4
and C
2
H
6
from DMC, C
2
H
4
from EC [35]. Additionally, HF is a toxic gas that can be produced during the reaction between LiPF
6
and H
2
O or through the further reactions of PF
5
. The electrolyte has been shown to have several more reactions with other components such as anode [39], cathode [40] and SEI layer [19]. The decomposition kinetics of EC/DMC/EA LiPF
6
and EC/DMC LiPF
6
electrolytes are presented in Table 1. It should be noted that reaction 1 is ignored for EC/DMC LiPF
6
electrolyte.

As discussed in Section 1, CMC and PVDF binders are widely used and therefore, the thermal decomposition of these two materials was studied here. Kriston et al. [19] performed TGA at 10
o
C/min for CMC binder. The TGA data revealed a one-step reaction, as analysed in Refs. [9,35], indicating a reaction between the Li particles from the electrode and the binder at elevated temperatures, e.g., 260 °C [9], resulting in the production of H
2
. The kinetic of this reaction is presented in Table 1.

The decomposition of PVDF binder was studied by Li et al. [29] and Rehwoldt et al. [41] using TGA at a heating rate of 10
o
C/min. In Ref. [29] the peak mass loss was triggered at ∼ 400 °C and H
2
production was observed [9]. Then, no further mass loss was observed after 700 °C. As analysed in Refs. [29,41] and reviewed in Ref. [35], the decomposition kinetic of PVDF binder is presented in Table 1.

The generally accepted decomposition reactions in the condensed phase of battery components were discussed in this section. However, the reactions occurring during thermal runaway are not limited to these. Additional reactions between components and their reaction products have been studied in the literature. For instance, reactions between the electrolyte and moisture (H
2
O), the reaction between the graphite anode and HF gas, chain reactions at the SEI layer, and reactions between the CMC binder and the graphite anode have been discussed in Refs. [9,19,39,42]. The current study focused on the primary, generally accepted reactions, with these topics falling beyond its scope and therefore were not included.

For each component, the mass yield/loss rates presented in the third column of Table 1 constitute a system of ODEs with temperature as the independent variable. To solve the ODEs, the initial condition for the consumed species was set at

Genetic Algorithms have been widely employed in the literature to estimate unknown parameters by fitting the mass loss of components to experimental TGA data, as demonstrated by Rein et al. [32], Huang and Rein [33], Zanoni et al. [44], Alinejad et al. [45] and Hou et al. [46]. A genetic algorithm (GA) is a derivative-free global optimisation method inspired by Darwin’s theory of survival of the fittest [47,48]. Within a GA, candidate solutions represent individuals in a population that evolve over time within a predetermined environment. Here, the GA function of MATLAB was used to minimise the mean absolute error of calculated mass loss and mass-loss rate of every component against the corresponding experimental data as [46]

The differences in thermal behaviour between the cathode systems (Graphite/LCO, Graphite/LFP, and Graphite/NMC) stem from variations in the interplay between cathode-induced thermal instability and anode decomposition. The LFP system exhibits the lowest mass loss and a more gradual decomposition profile, indicating superior thermal stability compared to LCO and NMC. By contrast, the NMC system showed the fastest and the highest decomposition. The modelling results align closely with experimental data, as shown by the agreement between simulation outputs and experimental measurements (Fig. 3). However, the decomposition in NMC system may include further reactions. Optimised kinetic parameters and modelling errors are detailed in Tables 3 and 4, respectively.

The experimental data for separators were obtained from LG, A123, and Panasonic cells paired with LCO, LFP, and NCA cathodes, respectively, referred to as LG/LCO, A123/LFP, and Panasonic/NCA. The thermal decomposition of the separators was modelled using a single-step kinetic framework for LG/LCO and Panasonic/NCA and a two-step kinetic for A123/LFP. The kinetics are outlined in Table 1. For the single-step model, only reaction number 1 was considered. The comparison between model predictions and experimental results is presented in Fig. 4. As shown in the first and last rows of Fig. 4, the single-step thermal degradation of polypropylene, the primary component of the separators, is responsible for the observed mass loss. As seen in the middle row of Fig. 4, the primary decomposition of polypropylene happened around 150 °C and the following reaction of by-product happened around 200 °C–250 °C. In all cases, the modelled one- and two-step kinetics effectively captured the decomposition behaviours, as demonstrated by their close alignment with the experimental data.

In summary, polypropylene degradation is the dominant mechanism driving separator mass loss across all systems. While the LG and Panasonic separators are well-described by the single-step kinetic model, the A123 separator presents additional complexity due to the apparent presence of two reactions. The thermal stability trends observed are consistent with the cathode system: LG/LCO demonstrates the highest stability with decomposition initiation around 100 °C, followed by A123/LFP and Panasonic/NCA, reflecting the interplay between separator degradation and cathode-induced thermal effects. The optimised kinetic parameters are presented in Table 3, with modelling errors summarised in Table 4.

The thermal stability of the electrolytes decreases in the order EC/DMC > EC/DMC/EA. EC/DMC demonstrates greater resistance to decomposition, characterised by fewer low-temperature pathways and a more gradual mass loss. In contrast, EC/DMC/EA exhibits increased reactivity due to the presence of EA, which promotes additional reactions at lower temperatures. In summary, the thermal decomposition of EC/DMC and EC/DMC/EA electrolytes is governed by evaporation, organic solvent breakdown, and salt degradation. The kinetic parameters and the modelling errors are presented in Tables 3 and 4, respectively.

As presented in Table 4, the optimisation errors ranged from 0.039 % for the SEI layer to 1.531 % for the EC/DMC electrolyte, indicating the good performance of the GA. Additionally, the algorithm performed well in predicting reaction temperatures, with corresponding errors ranging from 0.51 % for the LFP cathode to 11.07 % for the EC/DMC/EA electrolyte.

This study focused on the 18650-type cells with LCO, LFP, and NMC cathodes at 100 % SOC, but the models can be extended to other chemistries or silicon-based anodes and also to further conditions, such as dynamic SOC cycling and environmental temperature variations. These extensions would require recalibration using experimental TGA/DSC data specific to these materials and conditions to capture their unique thermal behaviours.

In this section, two cases were analysed to validate the optimised parameters and demonstrate the application of the resulting models. In the validation case, a set of decomposition models presented in Section 4 was employed to simulate the mass loss of a full cell during thermal runaway. This analysis required the mass of each battery component. While the mass breakdown of 18650-type lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) had been investigated in several previous studies, such as those referenced in [9], the reported data typically included only major components — such as the anode, cathode, and separator — without accounting for all components involved in thermal runaway. In the literature, He et al. [20] conducted a detailed mass breakdown of 18650-type cells, reporting the mass of both thermally active and inactive components. The masses of the components, their mass ratios relative to the total mass of the cell, and the decomposition models employed are presented in Table 5.

The total mass-loss rate of the full cell is calculated as
(20)

+

ṁ

where “FC” refers to the full cell. Fig. 8(a) synthesises the component-specific thermal decomposition behaviours modelled in Figs. 2–7, using the decomposition models and mass contributions presented in Tables 3 and 5, respectively. This alignment enables a comprehensive understanding of the thermal response of a fully assembled 18650-type cell at 100% SOC.

The cathode active material, which contributes 32.88% of the cell mass and is modelled in Fig. 5, represents the largest single contributor and plays a dominant role in thermal decomposition. Its exothermic reactions significantly influence the total heat generation, as reflected in Fig. 8(a). The graphite anode, contributing 21.64% of the cell mass and modelled in Fig. 3, also has a substantial impact. Its decomposition contributes to early mass loss and thermal instability, particularly when paired with NMC cathodes. The electrolyte, which accounts for 17.73% of the mass and is analysed in Fig. 6, drives critical decomposition reactions. These reactions release heat and gases that exacerbate thermal runaway. Similarly, the SEI layer, contributing 3.8% of the mass and modelled in Fig. 2, triggers initial decomposition, initiating the cascade of exothermic reactions observed in Fig. 8(a). Other components, such as the separator, which contributes 3.14% of the mass and is modelled in Fig. 4, and the binders (CMC and PVDF), which together account for 2.01% of the mass and are modelled in Fig. 7, contribute to secondary thermal effects. Although their mass fractions are relatively small, their decomposition accelerates heat generation and influences the timing of thermal events.

By integrating the mass ratios from Table 5 with the modelling results from Figs. 2–7, Fig. 8(a) effectively represents the cumulative thermal decomposition behaviour of the cell. The deviations observed between the modelling results and the experimental data may be attributed to various factors. Variations in cell design, material composition, and manufacturing processes significantly complicate the generalisation of mass data used in thermal decomposition models. Simplifications in the decomposition pathways of battery components and the overall thermal runaway process further contribute to these inaccuracies. For example, thermal runaway is sometimes associated with electrolyte leakage, as highlighted in studies such as Feng et al. [49], which was not considered in the current study. Similarly, the distinct thermal behaviour of the anode was treated as a single-step reaction, leading to the neglect of certain phenomena observed in experimental measurements. In practical scenarios, the interactions between components are coupled; for instance, the decomposition of the SEI layer can accelerate electrolyte breakdown due to the exothermic reactions involved. Additionally, anode degradation can exacerbate cathode reactions, as noted in studies on thermal runaway propagation [9]. To overcome these limitations, decomposition models must incorporate cell-specific mass breakdowns and account for multi-step reaction pathways and coupled interactions between components. Validation using experimental data from full cells under diverse operating and abuse conditions is crucial to ensuring predictive accuracy. Additionally, sensitivity analyses can help identify and refine critical parameters, enhancing the robustness of the model.

The overall performance of the model closely matches the experimental data, with only a 5 % difference in the predicted residue compared to the experimental measurements of Liu et al. [50]. This discrepancy may be attributed to particle ejection in the experiments by Liu et al. [50]. The mass of ejected particles has been reported in the literature. For instance, Zhang et al. [51] observed that 4 g of particles were ejected from an 18650-type cell with an NMC cathode at 50 % SOC, accounting for 8.5 % of the total cell mass (47 g in Ref. [51]). Similarly, Wang et al. [52] demonstrated that the ejected particles comprised various materials, including aluminum, copper, phosphorus, and lithium, indicating that particle formation results from overall reactions rather than individual components. As a result, their effect is reflected in the total mass loss rather than in the mass loss of specific components. Since the current work focuses on individual components, particle formation was not included in this study. For such modelling, readers are referred to Ref. [1].

The application of the decomposition model could be in the electrochemical-thermal modelling of battery charge/discharge cycling. As an example, cycling of an 18650-type LIB with specifications presented in Table 6 was modelled using the PyBaMM solver [53].

The DFN model was employed with the parameters obtained by He et al. [54] and the convective heat transfer coefficient was set at 2 W/(m2 K). The cyclic test was defined similarly to that of Zhuo et al. [55] to closely resemble experimental conditions. With the initial SOC of 0 % , the test was defined as
(I) Charge at 2.0 C until 4.3 V,
(II) Hold at 4.3 V until C/50,
(III) Rest for 1.0 h,
(IV) Discharge at 2.0 C until 3.0 V,
(V) Hold at 3.0 V until C/50,
(VI) Rest for 1.0 h,

for 20 cycles. The cycling modelling was performed for two cases: with and without thermal decomposition. The components involved in the early stages of thermal runaway included the electrolyte, anode, and separator. Here, the decomposition of the anode and separator was modelled as a reduction in thickness, while the decomposition of the electrolyte was modelled as a reduction in molar fraction. Since the dynamic mesh is not supported in the PyBaMM solver, the final thickness/concentration reductions were considered over the temperature range in the cycles. Hence, the thickness of the anode and separator was set to 0.98 and 0.97 of the original values, respectively, and the initial electrolyte concentration was set to 0.85 of the original value. Although, validated surrogate models or stepwise reductions in material properties could approximate the effects of thickness/concentration changes in the absence of dynamic mesh or properties support in PyBaMM. While not as precise as dynamic mesh modelling, this approach can provide actionable insights for thermal behaviour predictions. Fig. 8 shows the voltage and temperature over 20 cycles. As observed, the changes assumed by thermal decomposition caused the temperature and voltage values to advance by approximately 0.07 C in the last cycle compared to the original case. This observation could be considered in battery management systems for predicting thermal runaway and the models could be combined with machine learning techniques such as convolutional and graph neural networks, to enable more precise TR prediction by analysing the cell performance and the reference experiments or simulations [56,57]. This study highlights that integrating the actual time- or temperature-dependant mass loss of the battery components in the PyBaMM solver would require a moving mesh, which could be explored in future work.

With applications in cell design and material selection, decomposition models provide insights into component-specific mass loss. They support prioritising materials with lower decomposition enthalpies or reduced gas generation, as well as designing thermally stable components. Integrating these models into thermal management simulations enables the prediction of potential thermal hotspots and the optimisation of cooling strategies. Additionally, the models can be utilised to simulate defects in the components.

This study developed models for the thermal decomposition of 18650-type battery components at 100 % SOC during thermal runaway. The components analysed included the SEI layer, anode, separator, cathode, electrolyte, and binder. Decomposition kinetics and experimental TGA data were sourced from the literature. The Arrhenius correlation was applied to describe reaction rates, and an inverse modelling approach using a Genetic Algorithm was employed to estimate the kinetic parameters and stoichiometric coefficients. The GA minimised calculation errors in mass loss and mass-loss rate by fitting model predictions to experimental data. Optimisation errors ranged from 0.039 % for the SEI layer to 1.531 % for the EC/DMC electrolyte, demonstrating the algorithm’s high performance. The GA also showed good accuracy in predicting reaction temperatures, with errors from 0.51 % for the LFP cathode to 11.07 % for the EC/DMC/EA electrolyte. Upon meeting optimisation criteria such as function and constraint tolerances, the best parameter values and ranges were reported. The validated models were then applied to simulate the thermal decomposition of a full cell during thermal runaway. The models successfully predicted mass loss but exhibited some errors, which were attributed to factors such as the exclusion of particle ejection effects. Additionally, the early stages of thermal runaway — including the thermal decomposition of the anode and separator — were simulated under charge/discharge cycling conditions. After 20 cycles, temperature and voltage shifts of 0.07 C were observed in the final cycle, which could be linked to SEI layer formation. These effects suggest potential applications in battery management systems (BMSs) for predicting thermal runaway.

The novelty of this work lies in the development of thermal decomposition models capable of calculating mass loss in LIB components and their integration into electrochemical-thermal simulations during thermal runaway. These models can be used independently or integrated into full-cell simulations. Moreover, their integration into electrochemical-thermal simulations highlights their utility in a BMS, particularly for predicting thermal runaway events.

While effective for current battery chemistries, the models need recalibration for safer or emerging components. For instance, alternative binder materials, such as alginate or ionic-liquid-based polymers, could improve safety by reducing thermal decomposition impacts compared to PVDF and CMC and future work could explore their thermal stability. Incorporating SOC-dependent kinetics, ageing effects, further chemical interactions between the components, and dynamic conditions will enhance the models’ generalisability. Achieving this may require revised formulations and further development of numerical solvers such as PyBaMM. Figure S.2 of supplementary data, Appendix A, summarises the future outlook associated with this research.

Hosein Sadeghi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Francesco Restuccia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
